categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Umayyad province to independent caliphate — the Córdoba dynasty

entries.label: Tariq ibn Ziyad — the conquest of Hispania
entries.hint: Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses from Africa with 7,000 troops in April 711 — within 7 years nearly the entire peninsula is under Muslim control; the independent Christian states are confined to the Asturian mountains, but the Christian population across al-Andalus remains the demographic majority for generations, living as Mozarabs under the dhimma system.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (written in al-Andalus by a Christian, 754 CE): the most contemporary account — primary source. The earliest al-Andalus coin (716 CE, dinar): Archaeological Museum of Madrid — bears 'al-Andalus' in Arabic and 'Span' in Latin on the same coin. Collins (1989), The Arab Conquest of Spain: the standard modern study. Note: the confinement of independent Christian *states* to the north should not be confused with the Christian *population* of al-Andalus — Mozarab Christians were the demographic majority within the Umayyad emirate during the 8th and early 9th centuries, as documented by Eulogius of Córdoba and the Martyrs of Córdoba episode.

entries.label: Abd al-Rahman I — founding the independent emirate
entries.hint: The last surviving Umayyad prince flees the Abbasid massacre in Damascus and reaches al-Andalus — defeats the Abbasid governor and establishes an independent emirate; he begins the Great Mosque of Córdoba in 785; his dynasty will rule for 275 years.
entries.note: Abd al-Rahman I's flight and arrival: Ibn al-Athir (12th c.) and al-Maqqari (17th c.) drawing on earlier sources. Great Mosque of Córdoba construction begun 785: contemporary building accounts lost but the mosque itself is the primary source; first expansion documented. Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal: the standard modern survey.

entries.label: Abd al-Rahman III — Caliph of the West
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III declares himself Caliph on 16 January 929 — the first Caliph of al-Andalus, challenging both the Abbasids and Fatimids; he builds Madinat al-Zahra (begun 936) as his palace city; his court exchanges embassies with the Byzantine Emperor, Otto I and the Holy Roman Empire.
entries.note: Proclamation of the Caliphate 929: contemporary chronicle by ʿĪsā al-Rāzī (annals 971–975, the court chronicler). Madinat al-Zahra (begun 936): archaeological site west of Córdoba (extant, UNESCO). The Byzantine embassy: documented in Constantine Porphyrogenitus's De Ceremoniis (Constantinople) and in Arabic sources. Wasserstein (1985), The Caliphate in the West: the standard study.

entries.label: Al-Hakam II — the scholar-caliph
entries.hint: Al-Hakam II builds the Córdoba caliphate's greatest library — 400,000 volumes; he personally annotates manuscripts and corresponds with scholars across the Islamic world; the Dioscorides translation had been commissioned under his father Abd al-Rahman III (the Greek manuscript arrived as a Byzantine diplomatic gift c.948–949, translated by a team including Hasdai ibn Shaprut and the monk Nicolas who died in 961, the year al-Hakam ascended).
entries.note: The library of 400,000 volumes: al-Maqqari. The Dioscorides gift: sent to Abd al-Rahman III c.948–949 — documented in Byzantine sources (Constantine Porphyrogenitus's De Ceremoniis) and Arabic sources. The translation team: Hasdai ibn Shaprut and the Byzantine monk Nicolas — Nicolas died 961, the year al-Hakam II came to the throne. Al-Hakam's personal annotations: reported in sources. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate of Córdoba.

entries.label: Almanzor (al-Mansur) — the regent who eclipsed the caliph
entries.hint: Ibn Abi Amir al-Mansur (Almanzor) rules as regent for the child caliph Hisham II for 24 years; he leads 57 documented military campaigns, sacks Santiago de Compostela (997); his death in 1002 is followed by six years of stable rule under his capable son Abd al-Malik al-Muzaffar; the collapse is triggered in 1008–1009 when a second son, Abd al-Rahman Sanchuelo, rashly claims succession to the caliphate itself, provoking the Umayyad loyalist revolt that begins the fitna.
entries.note: Almanzor's campaigns: Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib (13th c. but drawing on contemporary sources). The sack of Santiago 997: documented in both Arabic and Christian sources. His 57 campaigns: listed in Arabic sources. Kennedy (1996) pp.107–120. The irony: the Christian kingdom's most sacred site destroyed by a Muslim regent — the bells carried to Córdoba as lamp holders.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The conquest, the Battle of Tours, and the northern frontier

entries.label: Umayyad advance into France — Battle of Tours/Poitiers
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi leads an Umayyad army deep into Francia — Charles Martel defeats him at Tours/Poitiers in October 732; the battle's importance has been debated (was it decisive?) but it marks the northernmost limit of Muslim advance in Western Europe.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (primary, by a Christian in al-Andalus): calls it the 'Battle of the Court of Martyrs'. Fredegar's Chronicle continuation: Frankish perspective. Charles Martel's role: documented. The battle's significance debate: Riche (1993) vs Collins (1989) — both use the same thin primary sources. The Muslim withdrawal: subsequent Arabic sources attribute it to raiding rather than permanent conquest.

entries.label: Berber Revolt — the crisis that reshaped al-Andalus
entries.loc: Al-Andalus (Berber-held zones in the north and centre)
entries.hint: The Berbers — who formed the bulk of the conquering army but were treated as second-class citizens by the Arab elite — revolt across al-Andalus and North Africa in 739; the revolt shatters the early emirate, forces Damascus to send Syrian military divisions (junds) to restore order, and permanently alters the demographic and tribal landscape of al-Andalus.
entries.note: Ibn Abd al-Hakam, Futuh Misr (9th c.): primary source for the Berber revolt. Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib: detailed account of the jund settlements. Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal pp.25–35: the standard modern analysis. The jund system (five Syrian military divisions settled in different regions): fundamental to al-Andalus's later tribal geography — documented in administrative sources. Collins (1989), The Arab Conquest of Spain ch.6.

entries.label: Roncesvalles — the ambush that created the Song of Roland
entries.hint: Charlemagne's retreating army is ambushed in the Roncesvalles Pass — the historical attackers are Basques, not Muslims, but the Chanson de Roland (c.1100) transforms them into a Muslim army; the gap between the historical event and its literary transformation is a primary example of medieval mythmaking.
entries.note: Einhard, Vita Karoli Magni (c.830): primary source — explicitly says Basques, names Roland as one of the dead. The Annales Regni Francorum: contemporary but vague. The Chanson de Roland (c.1100): BnF manuscript (Oxford copy). The transformation from Basques to Muslims: the clearest example of how the Reconquista narrative reshaped historical memory. Short (1990), La Chanson de Roland.

entries.label: Revolt of the Suburb — the largest urban uprising of the Emirate
entries.hint: Under Emir al-Hakam I, the working population of Córdoba's southern suburb rises in mass revolt — the uprising is crushed with extreme violence; the suburb is razed, hundreds of leaders are crucified on the riverbank, and over 20,000 Andalusis are expelled; some of these exiles go on to conquer Alexandria and establish the Emirate of Crete.
entries.note: Ibn al-Qutiyya, Tarikh iftitah al-Andalus (10th c.): primary narrative source. Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments): additional details. The exile to Alexandria and Crete: documented in Arabic sources — the Andalusian exiles seize Alexandria c.814–815 and Crete c.824. Kennedy (1996) pp.67–72. The razed suburb: confirmed archaeologically — no 9th-century occupation layer on the south bank. This event directly precedes the 'Martyrs of Córdoba' generation.

entries.label: The Martyrs of Córdoba — Christian provocation under Muslim rule
entries.hint: A group of Christians in Córdoba deliberately seek martyrdom by publicly insulting Islam — 48 are executed between 850 and 859; Eulogius of Córdoba documents them; the episode reveals the tensions within the Mozarab community between accommodation and resistance to Muslim rule.
entries.note: Eulogius of Córdoba, Memoriale Sanctorum (851–858) and Documentum Martyriale (c.857): primary source — Eulogius was himself eventually executed (859). The Arabic-language response: the Qadi Ibn Ziyad's counter-arguments (preserved in Christian sources). Coope (1995), The Martyrs of Córdoba: the standard modern study. The episode documents both Christian-Muslim tension and the substantial Mozarab community.

entries.label: Ibn Hafsun and Bobastro — the rebel state that nearly broke the Emirate
entries.hint: Umar ibn Hafsun, a muwallad (neo-Muslim of native Iberian descent), establishes a rebel state at the mountain fortress of Bobastro and controls much of southern al-Andalus for nearly 40 years — at times converting to Christianity; the emirate's desperate need to crush this rebellion drives Abd al-Rahman III's declaration of the Caliphate (929) as a legitimacy move.
entries.note: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments, vol.II and III): primary source. Ibn Hafsun's conversion to Christianity: documented in Arabic sources — his body was exhumed after his death and found buried in Christian fashion. The Bobastro fortress: partially excavated — a church confirmed within the fortifications. Acién Almansa (1994), Entre el feudalismo y el Islam: the standard modern study. Kennedy (1996) pp.85–105: analyses the connection between the rebellion and the Caliphate's proclamation.

entries.label: Battle of Simancas — the Caliphate's greatest military defeat
entries.hint: King Ramiro II of León crushes the forces of Abd al-Rahman III at Simancas on 6 August 939 — the caliphate's most catastrophic military defeat; it permanently halts northward expansion, forces the Caliph to stop leading campaigns personally, and solidifies the Duero as the permanent frontier between al-Andalus and the Christian kingdoms.
entries.note: Chronicle of Sampiro (c.1000): primary Christian source for the battle. Arabic sources: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis Vol.V (the most detailed account) — published García Gómez (1967). The battle's date 6 Aug 939: confirmed in multiple sources. The permanent frontier effect: Kennedy (1996) pp.100–102. The Caliph's cessation of personal command after Simancas: documented in subsequent annals — he never led another campaign.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Islamic governance, the dhimma system and Umayyad administration

entries.label: The dhimma — legal status of Christians and Jews under Islam
entries.hint: The Muslim conquest establishes the dhimma system — Christians and Jews are 'People of the Book', protected but subject to the jizya tax; the Pact of Tudmir (713) is the primary documented example, giving the Christian lord Theodemir (Tudmir) his lands in exchange for submission.
entries.note: Pact of Tudmir (713 CE): Arabic text preserved in al-Dabbi (13th c.) — translated Constable (1997), Medieval Iberia: Readings from Christian, Muslim and Jewish Sources. This is the best-documented early Muslim-Christian accommodation agreement in al-Andalus. Collins (1989) pp.38–42. The dhimma's practical application: documented in later legal sources and administrative records.

entries.label: Caliphate of Córdoba — administrative structure
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III creates a sophisticated caliphal administration — diwan (ministry) system, tax collection through amilship, royal tiraz (textile workshops), mint producing the first al-Andalus gold dinars; the annals of ʿĪsā al-Rāzī (971–975) document the daily operations of this court.
entries.note: ʿĪsā al-Rāzī's court annals (971–975): the extraordinary source that records daily court events from June 971 to July 975 — analysed in Manzano Moreno (2023), The Court of the Caliphate of al-Andalus (Edinburgh): the standard study using this source. The first al-Andalus gold dinars (929): documented — marking Abd al-Rahman III's caliphate claim. The Madinat al-Zahra archaeology: extant site.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Islam, Mozarab Christianity, Judaism and the question of convivencia

entries.label: Mozarab community — Christians under Muslim rule
entries.hint: The Mozarab Christians continue their faith under Muslim rule — they pay the jizya, maintain churches, copy manuscripts and produce scholars like Eulogius; the term 'Mozarab' (from Arabic mustarib, 'one who adopts Arab ways') documents the cultural integration that shaped a distinctive Iberian Christianity.
entries.note: Eulogius of Córdoba (died 859): primary source for Mozarab life. The Mozarabic manuscripts (9th–10th c.): Real Academia de la Historia. The Beatus manuscripts (commentaries on the Apocalypse, made in Christian monasteries under Muslim rule): UNESCO Memory of the World. Simonet (1897), Historia de los Mozárabes de España: the older comprehensive study.

entries.label: Great Mosque of Córdoba — the architectural primary source
entries.hint: The Great Mosque of Córdoba (begun 785) is expanded by every major ruler — Abd al-Rahman I, II, III, al-Hakam II and Almanzor each add sections; the building itself is a chronological primary source documenting caliphal ambition and wealth across 200 years.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). Construction phases: documented in Arabic sources and confirmed archaeologically. The horseshoe arches reusing Visigothic and Roman columns: physical evidence of cultural layering. Al-Hakam II's expansion (961–976): the most ornate — the mihrab mosaics made by Byzantine craftsmen (documented in Arabic sources). Dodds (1992), Architecture and Ideology in Early Medieval Spain: the standard art-historical study.

entries.label: Hasdai ibn Shaprut — the Jewish vizier of Córdoba
entries.hint: Hasdai ibn Shaprut serves as Abd al-Rahman III's physician, diplomat and de facto foreign minister — he corresponds with the Byzantine Emperor and the Khazar King in Hebrew; his career is the clearest documented example of Jewish political participation at the summit of an Islamic state.
entries.note: Hasdai's letter to the Khazar King Joseph (c.960): Hebrew text preserved in multiple manuscripts; published Kobler (1978), Letters of Jews Through the Ages. His diplomatic role: documented in Arabic sources (al-Maqqari) and confirmed by Byzantine records. The Byzantine-Córdoba correspondence he handled: cross-referenced in Byzantine sources. Mann (1920), The Jews in Egypt and Palestine: the older standard study.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Andalusian architecture, Beatus manuscripts and court culture

entries.label: Ziryab — the musician who transformed Andalusian culture
entries.hint: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Nafi (Ziryab) arrives in Córdoba from Baghdad in 822 — he is the greatest musician of the medieval Mediterranean, establishes the first music conservatory in Europe, adds a fifth string to the lute, codifies Andalusian music theory, and introduces new fashions, cuisine and etiquette to the Córdoba court.
entries.note: Ziryab's arrival and role: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (10th-c. court chronicle); al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib (drawing on Ibn Hayyan). The fifth string added to the oud: documented in Arabic musical theory sources. Shiloah (1995), Music in the World of Islam: the standard study. Ziryab's influence on Andalusian (flamenco-precursor) music: documented in the maqamat tradition.

entries.label: Madinat al-Zahra — the palace city of the caliphs
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III builds Madinat al-Zahra as a new capital city — 1.5 sq km, housing the caliphal court, government offices, gardens and mosque; partially destroyed in civil war (1010) and abandoned; ongoing excavations since 1910 make it the richest archaeological site of Islamic Spain.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 2018). Construction begun 936–940: documented in Arabic sources (al-Maqqari). The reception halls: excavated and partially restored. Vallejo Triano (2010), La ciudad califal de Madinat al-Zahra: the standard archaeological study. The deliberate siting: to challenge the Fatimid and Abbasid caliphates — political ideology made architecture.

entries.label: Pyxis of al-Mughira — Caliphal secular expression in ivory
entries.loc: Madinat al-Zahra (made); Musée du Louvre, Paris (held)
entries.hint: The Pyxis of al-Mughira (968 AD) is the masterpiece of Caliphal ivory carving — a cylindrical ivory box made for Abd al-Rahman III's son al-Mughira, carved with court scenes of falconers, musicians playing lutes, and date-palm harvest; the secular, non-religious iconography is a primary visual record of Caliphal courtly life that architectural decoration could not provide.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Pyxis of al-Mughira (968 AD): Musée du Louvre, Paris (OA 4068). Inscription: 'Made for al-Mughira, son of the Commander of the Faithful, may God have mercy on him' — dated by the inscription to 968, confirmed by al-Hakam II's reign. Ferrandis Torres (1935–1940), Marfiles y azabaches españoles: the foundational catalogue. Shalem (1996), Islam Christianized: the standard modern study of Caliphal ivory carving. The Louvre acquisition: 1898. The secular scenes contrast with restrictions on religious figurative art — primary evidence for Andalusian courtly aesthetics.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Córdoba as the intellectual capital of Western Europe

entries.label: Al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis) — the father of surgery
entries.hint: Abu al-Qasim al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis) is al-Hakam II's court physician — his Kitab al-Tasrif (The Method of Medicine, 30 volumes) contains the first illustrated surgical treatise; 200 surgical instruments are described and depicted; it remained the standard European surgical text for 500 years.
entries.note: Kitab al-Tasrif (c.1000 CE): multiple Arabic manuscripts; Vol.30 (the surgical treatise) translated into Latin by Gerard of Cremona c.1150. Earliest manuscripts: Biblioteca Medicea Laurenziana, Florence. Hamarneh & Sonnedecker (1963), A Pharmaceutical View of Abulcasis al-Zahrawi: the standard English study. The Latin translation's influence: documented in European medical manuscript transmission.

entries.label: Almanzor purges al-Hakam II's library — state censorship
entries.loc: Córdoba (library at Madinat al-Zahra and palace)
entries.hint: To win the support of conservative religious jurists (ulema) and consolidate his regency, Almanzor publicly purges al-Hakam II's 400,000-volume library — burning or disposing of volumes of philosophy, astronomy, logic and music; the act of state censorship directly follows the praise of that library and is the other side of the caliphate's intellectual story.
entries.note: The library purge: documented in Arabic sources including al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib (drawing on earlier sources). Almanzor's purge is dated to the first years of his effective regency (c.976–978). Kennedy (1996) pp.107–110: the standard analysis. The irony: Almanzor who destroyed the library is also the patron of many scholars — the purge was politically rather than intellectually motivated. The dispersal of volumes: some survived in private collections — documented in later bibliographic sources.

entries.label: Ibn Hayyan — the greatest Andalusian historian
entries.hint: Ibn Hayyan is the greatest historian of al-Andalus — his Muqtabis (an annalistic history of the Umayyad period) and Matin (a contemporary chronicle) are the primary narrative sources for the caliphate at its height; large portions survive only in later quotations.
entries.note: Muqtabis: partially surviving in multiple volumes. Vol. II (reign of Abd al-Rahman II): Madrid Real Academia de la Historia. Vol. V (reign of Abd al-Rahman III): published by García Gómez (1967). Matin: largely lost; fragments quoted in later authors. Kennedy (1996) uses Ibn Hayyan throughout. The partial survival: a result of the sack of Córdoba's libraries (1009–1031).

entries.label: Maliki fiqh — the orthodox framework of Andalusi law
entries.hint: The Maliki school of Islamic jurisprudence — founded by Malik ibn Anas of Medina — becomes the official legal orthodoxy of al-Andalus under Hisham I (796); its conservative reliance on the practice of Medina and judicial consensus (ijma) shapes all public life; its restriction of rationalist philosophy triggers the counter-movements of Ibn Masarra, Ibn Hazm and eventually Averroës.
entries.note: The adoption of Malikism under Hisham I (796): documented in Ibn al-Faradi's biographical dictionary of Andalusi scholars. Malik's al-Muwatta (the foundational text): multiple manuscripts; translated Bewley (1989). Fierro (1987), La heterodoxia en al-Andalus durante el período omeya: the standard study of Maliki orthodoxy and its opponents. The Maliki school's institutional dominance: documented in the qadi (judge) appointment records analysed by Marín (1992). This entry explains why rationalist philosophy was dangerous in al-Andalus — essential context for Averroës's exile.

entries.label: Ibn Masarra — the first Andalusi mystical philosopher
entries.hint: Ibn Masarra (869–931) is the pioneer of esoteric philosophy and proto-Sufism in al-Andalus — he introduces Neo-Platonic and mystical ideas challenging Maliki orthodoxy, gathers a secret school in the Sierra de Córdoba, and lays the intellectual and metaphysical foundations for Ibn Arabi three centuries later; his works were condemned and burned.
entries.note: Ibn Masarra's works: only fragments survive — his Risalat al-I'tibar and Kitab al-Huruf partially preserved in later quotations. Asín Palacios (1914), Ibn Masarra y su escuela: the foundational study (Spanish). Stroumsa (1992), Freethinkers of Medieval Islam: contextualises Ibn Masarra within the rationalist underground. The burning of his books: documented in Islamic biographical sources. The connection to Ibn Arabi: argued by Asín Palacios (1931), El islam cristianizado — the philosophical lineage. His Sierra de Córdoba school: documented in biographic dictionaries.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: The agricultural revolution and the wealth of al-Andalus

entries.label: Arab agricultural revolution — new crops transform Iberia
entries.hint: The Muslim conquest introduces new crops and irrigation technology to Iberia — cotton, sugar cane, rice, saffron, citrus fruits, artichokes, aubergines and hard wheat (durum) are brought from the Islamic world; the Valencian huerta (irrigated garden) system is established.
entries.note: The Arabic agricultural manuals: Ibn Bassal's Libro de Agricultura (11th c.) — Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial; Abu'l-Khayr al-Ishbili's Kitab al-Filaha (12th c.). Watson (1983), Agricultural Innovation in the Early Islamic World: the standard study of the 'Arab agricultural revolution'. The Valencian irrigation system: still operating today — documented from Arabic sources and continued in use.

entries.label: Jizya and kharaj — the fiscal roots of social rebellion
entries.hint: The Umayyad state treasury relies on a dual-tax system — jizya (poll tax on non-Muslims) and kharaj (land tax); as mass Muwallad conversions to Islam exempt converts from the jizya, the emirs manipulate agricultural tax categories to compensate; this fiscal squeeze on converts and non-Arab Muslims is the economic root of the social rebellions documented in the Conflict category.
entries.note: The jizya and kharaj system in al-Andalus: documented in Ibn Hawqal, Kitab Surat al-Ard (10th c.) — primary geographical and fiscal source. The Muwallad tax problem: Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain pp.60–75 — analyses the connection between conversion waves and fiscal crisis. The Bobastro rebellion's fiscal dimension: Acién Almansa (1994) — the Muwallads' tax grievances explicitly documented. The dual-tax system: Sijpesteijn (2013), Shaping a Muslim State: the comparative Egyptian evidence for the same dynamics.

entries.label: Jizya, kharaj and the Muwallad fiscal crisis
entries.hint: The Umayyad state relies on a dual-tax system: jizya (poll tax on non-Muslims) and kharaj (land tax); mass conversions of native Hispano-Romans (Muwallads) threaten state revenue by removing jizya payers; the Emirs manipulate agricultural tax categories to compensate, creating structural fiscal injustice that directly sparks the Muwallad social rebellions documented in the Lives category.
entries.note: The jizya and kharaj system: documented in the Pact of Tudmir (713) — the earliest al-Andalus administrative document. The Muwallad conversion-revenue problem: analysed in Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal pp.44–60. The fiscal manipulation response: documented in Arabic administrative sources. Acién Almansa (1994), Entre el feudalismo y el Islam: explicitly connects the fiscal injustice to the Ibn Hafsun rebellion. The dual-tax system is the foundation of Umayyad political economy in al-Andalus.

entries.label: Córdoba at its peak — the largest city in Western Europe
entries.hint: At its 10th-century peak, Córdoba has a population of 100,000–300,000 (estimates vary widely) — the largest city in Western Europe by far; it has 3,000 mosques, 300 public baths, paved streets with lighting; the contrast with contemporary London or Paris (populations in the low thousands) is dramatic.
entries.note: Population estimates: al-Maqqari's figures are almost certainly exaggerated (500,000); modern estimates range 100,000–300,000 — documented in Kennedy (1996) pp.165–167. The 300 public baths: documented in Arabic sources. The comparison with northern Europe: Whitelock (1952) on Anglo-Saxon London. The uncertainty must be flagged: population figures in medieval sources are notoriously unreliable.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Diplomacy, translation and the transmission of knowledge

entries.label: The 716 coin — al-Andalus names itself
entries.hint: A dinar struck in 716 — just 5 years after the conquest — bears 'al-Andalus' in Arabic on one side and 'Span' in Latin on the other; the earliest attestation of the Arabic name for the territory; the bilingual coin documents the new political entity negotiating its identity in two scripts simultaneously.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: The 716 dinar: Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid. The 'Span' inscription: the earliest dated use of this Latin form. Published in Miles (1950), The Coinage of the Umayyads of Spain. Cited in Wikipedia article on Al-Andalus as 'the earliest attestation of this Arab name'. The bilingual coin is the clearest single object documenting the conquest and naming.

entries.label: Radhanite trade network — Córdoba as western terminus of global commerce
entries.hint: Córdoba serves as the western terminus of the Radhanite Jewish merchant network — an extraordinary trans-Eurasian trade system connecting Spain to Baghdad, India and China; the Radhanites bring paper-making technology, silks, spices and Chinese goods westward; Hasdai ibn Shaprut (the Jewish vizier) is the network's Córdoba anchor.
entries.note: The Radhanite network: documented in Ibn Khordadbeh, Book of Roads and Kingdoms (c.870) — the primary source describing their four trans-continental routes. The Córdoba connection: documented through Hasdai ibn Shaprut's diplomatic correspondence. McCormick (2001), Origins of the European Economy ch.20: the standard modern study of the Radhanite network. The paper-making transmission: paper reaches al-Andalus c.900 — documented in manuscript production evidence.

entries.label: Radhanites and the muwashshah — Córdoba at the centre of global exchange
entries.loc: Córdoba (western terminus); Baghdad; India; China (Radhanite network)
entries.hint: Córdoba is the western terminus of the Radhanite Jewish trade network — linking Spain to Baghdad, India and China; simultaneously, Andalusi poets develop the muwashshah and zajal verse forms that introduce Romance vernacular endings (kharjas) into Arabic poetry — the most intimate linguistic exchange in the whole Iberian contact zone.
entries.note: The Radhanite network: Ibn Khordadbeh, Kitab al-Masalik (c.870) — the primary Arabic source describing their four trade routes through Spain. Constable (1994), Trade and Traders in Muslim Spain: the standard economic study. The muwashshah form: developed in al-Andalus c.9th–10th c. The kharjas: Romance vernacular lines embedded in Arabic/Hebrew poems — first published by Stern (1948). Monroe (1974), Hispano-Arabic Poetry: the standard English anthology. The kharjas are the earliest surviving examples of Romance vernacular poetry.

entries.label: Muwashshah and zajal — Romance vernacular enters Arabic poetry
entries.hint: The muwashshah (strophic poem) and zajal (colloquial variant) develop in al-Andalus as distinctive poetic forms — their final stanzas (kharjas) often switch to Romance vernacular or colloquial Arabic, embedding the earliest surviving Romance-language lyrics inside Arabic poetry; a unique literary exchange with no parallel elsewhere in the medieval world.
entries.note: The kharjas (Romance endings): first identified by Samuel Stern (1948) — Al-Andalus 13: the foundational discovery. The earliest examples: dated to the 11th century but the forms develop earlier. Monroe (1974), Hispano-Arabic Poetry: the standard anthology. The linguistic significance: the kharjas are the earliest surviving Romance-language lyric poetry — predating French troubadour poetry. Menocal (1987), The Arabic Role in Medieval Literary History: the standard study of the cross-cultural exchange.

entries.label: Córdoba and Byzantium — caliphal diplomacy
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III exchanges embassies with the Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII and with Otto I, King of East Francia (not yet Holy Roman Emperor — Otto received that title in 962, after Abd al-Rahman III's death in 961); the Byzantine mission brings a Greek Dioscorides manuscript; a Byzantine monk Nicolas is sent to assist translation.
entries.note: The Byzantine embassy c.948–952: Constantine Porphyrogenitus, De Ceremoniis; cross-referenced in ʿĪsā al-Rāzī's court annals. Otto I's status: King of East Francia at the time of the 953–954 exchange — he was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope John XII only on 2 February 962, after Abd al-Rahman III's death (Oct 961). The monk Nicolas's arrival: documented in Arabic sources. The Dioscorides manuscript: the primary object of the Byzantine exchange.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Al-Andalus in its Mediterranean and global context

entries.label: The Caliphate collapses — the taifa period begins
entries.hint: After Almanzor's death (1002) the caliphate fractures in civil war — the fitna (civil strife) of 1009–1031 sees Berber troops sack Madinat al-Zahra, al-Hakam II's library dispersed, and the caliphate dissolve into 23 taifa (faction) kingdoms; the greatest Islamic polity in the West disintegrates.
entries.note: The fitna documented: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments quoted in later sources). The sack of Madinat al-Zahra c.1009–1010: documented in Arabic chronicles. The dissolution of the caliphate 1031: formal deposition of Hisham III — documented. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate of Córdoba: the standard study. The dispersal of al-Hakam II's library: documented loss — a cultural catastrophe.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including the convivencia debate

entries.label: The convivencia debate — coexistence or oppression?
entries.hint: The degree of Jewish-Christian-Muslim coexistence in al-Andalus is the most contested question in Iberian medieval historiography — Américo Castro (1954) argued for a Golden Age of tolerance; Brian Catlos (2014) argues it was pragmatic coexistence under hierarchy; the dhimma documents legal inequality alongside cultural flowering.
entries.note: Medieval primary sources documenting the legal framework: Pact of Tudmir (713, AGI); dhimma provisions in al-Maqqari; Hasdai ibn Shaprut correspondence (BnF); AHN Inquisición records of Jewish and Christian communities. The HISTORIOGRAPHIC DEBATE anchor: Américo Castro, España en su Historia (1948) vs Brian Catlos (2014) — note these are 20th/21st-century frameworks, not medieval primary sources; the primary evidence is the Pact of Tudmir, the court annals, and the tax records. This entry treats the convivencia debate as a historiographic framework — the primary fact is that multiple religious communities co-existed under a structured legal hierarchy (dhimma), documented in medieval sources; the degree of tolerance is the contested interpretation.

entries.label: Muwallads — neo-Muslims and the discrimination that sparked revolt
entries.loc: Al-Andalus (throughout; concentrated in rural south and Málaga)
entries.hint: The Muwallads — native Hispano-Romans who converted to Islam — face systemic social discrimination from the Arab aristocracy who monopolise political offices; despite sincere conversion, Muwallads are excluded from power, taxed more heavily and treated as second-class Muslims; this unequal lived reality directly fuels the Ibn Hafsun rebellion (880–917), the longest internal revolt in al-Andalus history.
entries.note: The Muwallad social reality: documented in Ibn Hayyan's Muqtabis — the term muwallad itself implies adoptive or second-rank status. The discriminatory tax and administrative structures: analysed in Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal pp.60–75. The connection to Ibn Hafsun: Acién Almansa (1994), Entre el feudalismo y el Islam — argues the Bobastro rebellion is fundamentally a Muwallad social protest. The Arabic sources' own discomfort with the discrimination: noted in Tolan (2002), Saracens. The Muwallad social structure is the counterpart to the Mozarab and Jewish communities — a three-tier social reality documented across all contemporary sources.

entries.label: The Saqaliba — European slaves from trafficking to political power
entries.hint: The Saqaliba (literally 'Slavs') are European slaves imported into Córdoba through human trafficking networks — primarily of Slavic, Germanic and Frankish origin, many castrated to serve as palace eunuchs; some rise to become elite guards, administrators and court officials; during the fitna (civil war 1009–1031) several Saqaliba commanders establish their own taifa states.
entries.note: The Saqaliba in the caliphal court: documented in ʿĪsā al-Rāzī's court annals (971–975) and Ibn Hayyan's Muqtabis. The taifa states ruled by Saqaliba after 1031: Denia (Mujahid), Almería (Khayran), Tortosa (Labib) — documented in Ibn Idhari. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate: analyses the Saqaliba's political rise. The trafficking networks: documented in Frankish sources recording raids and slave trading. This is the most extreme documented case of CATEGORICAL SHIFT in the folio: from trafficked slave to sovereign ruler.

entries.label: Subh — the Basque concubine who ruled al-Andalus
entries.hint: Subh, a Basque concubine of Caliph al-Hakam II and mother of the child-caliph Hisham II, effectively co-rules al-Andalus with Almanzor during the regency — she controls the caliph's seal, manages correspondence and is documented wielding executive power as queen mother; her eventual break with Almanzor in 996 triggers a political crisis.
entries.note: Subh's role: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments) — she was al-Hakam II's concubine and Hisham II's mother, not Hisham II's concubine. Her management of the caliphal seal during Hisham II's minority: documented in contemporary sources. Her correspondence: some letters preserved. Rubiera Mata (1992), La mujer en al-Andalus. The break with Almanzor 996: documented. Subh is the best-documented woman in 10th-century Iberia, Muslim or Christian.

entries.label: Ibn Hazm — The Ring of the Dove
entries.hint: Ibn Hazm's Tawq al-Hamama (The Ring of the Dove, c.1022) is a treatise on love including documented accounts of same-sex desire — written in exile after the fall of Córdoba; it discusses love between men with the same analytical framework as heterosexual love, without the condemnatory framework of later Islamic jurisprudence.
entries.note: Tawq al-Hamama (c.1022): Arabic manuscripts; Arberry translation (1953), The Ring of the Dove. Ibn Hazm was a courtier of the last Umayyad caliph — writing from direct observation. Pérès (1953), La poésie andalouse en arabe classique: contextualises the homoerotic poetry tradition. The text's frank treatment of same-sex desire: noted by Boswell (1980), Christianity, Social Tolerance and Homosexuality (uses it as evidence for medieval Islamic tolerance).
